title: be going to (do)
desc: 계획된 의도 또는 근거 있는 예측
examples: Today, I'm going to show you my work routine as a YouTuber. Throughout this video, you'll learn some business English that you can use at the office and in different work-related situations. I've got a busy schedule today, so let's dive right in. I try to stay organized because it helps with my time management and productivity. First, I prioritize my tasks to make sure I'm on track to meet any deadlines. For my to-do list, I keep it pretty simple and use the tasks tab on Google Calendar. I have a weekly to-do list and every morning, I build my daily to-do list. I was supposed to record an MP3 file yesterday, but I missed the deadline and pushed it back to today. So now it's one of my top priorities.

Today, I also have to write two chapters of my book, update our free notion page, film at least one short and edit maybe three. And finally, I'm going to try and develop a lunch hour English worksheet. Next, I have to see what's on the agenda for today by check in Google Calendar. It looks like I have a Zoom meeting at 9.30am with Gem and in the afternoon, I have an offline meeting with my boss. Finally, I open up Gmail to check my emails. I always make sure to flag anything urgent. I've been in discussion with a new teacher that we'll be working with, so I have to follow up with him about his schedule. While I do this, I'll give you some tips on how to write a professional email. Step one is your greeting and which greeting you use really depends on your relationship with the person that you're writing to. If you have a close casual relationship with somebody, you might just use hey or hi. If you want to sound a little bit more professional, you could say deer, mister or misses. This is a very safe choice. It's professional and commonly used. Finally, one last option could be to whom it may concern. This one is used when your message is intended for larger, more general audiences. It's like you don't know who specifically is reading that email or document. I'm pretty close with Jacob, so I'm going to keep it short in casual and just say hi, Jacob. Step two is your opening line. This should be very short, but friendly. You could say something like thank you for your time yesterday or it was so nice seeing you the other day. If it's someone that you haven't seen for a while, you could also say I hope you've been well. One very professional and common option is I hope this email finds you well, which is what I'm going to use. Step three is to write your reason for the email. You can be a little bit more direct here. I'm writing because the purpose of this email is and then write why you're writing. I'm going to say I'm reaching out to follow up on our discussion about you potentially taking on a new class. I've drafted a proposed schedule and would like to share it with you for confirmation. Step four is to notify them if there are any attachments.

You can say I've attached something to this document. I'm going to say please find attached the schedule for next month. Step five is to include any requests that you have. This part is a little bit tricky because you need to be clear on what you want, but you also can't sound too pushy. In this email I need to ask Jacob if he will look over the schedule that I made and confirm it with me. I'm going to do this by saying could you review it at your earliest convenience? That's a great phrase to know for emails. At your earliest convenience, it's very polite. It just means when they have time and let me know if the times work for you. If anything means to be adjusted, please feel free to suggest alternatives. Feel free is also another great phrase to know. It's like telling the other person to be open and comfortable and honest. Feel free to let me know if I need to change anything. Step six is your closing. This might change depending on the content of your email. So if you ask them for a favor, you could simply say thank you or thanks in advance. If you're doing something for them, you might say don't hesitate to reach out if you need anything further or let me know if you need anything else. One closing that can be used in basically any situation is I look forward to hearing back from you or I'm looking forward to your reply. This is the one I'm going to use with Jacob because it's nice and professional. It also lets him know that I'm expecting something back from him. If you're emailing a friend or someone that you're very close with and you want to keep it super casual, you could say cheers, take care or talk to you soon. Some more professional options are best regards or all the best or simply thank you. For very official documents or formal emails, you can say sincerely. I'm asking Jacob for a favor so I'm going to just say thank you. Keep it simple. Finally, our last step. Step seven is your signature. You write your name and maybe your email address or your company info afterwards.

Definitely. We'll do. Thank you so much Gem and I'll talk to you again soon. That was a great meeting with Gem and now I'm going to record an MP3 file because that was at the top of my to-do list and I want to knock it out right away. I'll be recording an audio file of the script for our latest worksheet. Mike test, Mike test. I have to clear my throat.
My commute takes about 45 minutes to an hour one way so I'm going to be standing for a while.

If it's someone that you haven't seen for a while, you could also say I hope you've been well. One very professional and common option is I hope this email finds you well, which is what I'm going to use. Step three is to write your reason for the email. You can be a little bit more direct here. I'm writing because the purpose of this email is and then write why you're writing. I'm going to say I'm reaching out to follow up on our discussion about you potentially taking on a new class. I've drafted a proposed schedule and would like to share it with you for confirmation. Step four is to notify them if there are any attachments.

You can say I've attached something to this document. I'm going to say please find attached the schedule for next month. Step five is to include any requests that you have. This part is a little bit tricky because you need to be clear on what you want, but you also can't sound too pushy. In this email I need to ask Jacob if he will look over the schedule that I made and confirm it with me. I'm going to do this by saying could you review it at your earliest convenience? That's a great phrase to know for emails. At your earliest convenience, it's very polite. It just means when they have time and let me know if the times work for you. If anything means to be adjusted, please feel free to suggest alternatives. Feel free is also another great phrase to know. It's like telling the other person to be open and comfortable and honest. Feel free to let me know if I need to change anything. Step six is your closing. This might change depending on the content of your email. So if you ask them for a favor, you could simply say thank you or thanks in advance. If you're doing something for them, you might say don't hesitate to reach out if you need anything further or let me know if you need anything else. One closing that can be used in basically any situation is I look forward to hearing back from you or I'm looking forward to your reply. This is the one I'm going to use with Jacob because it's nice and professional. It also lets him know that I'm expecting something back from him. If you're emailing a friend or someone that you're very close with and you want to keep it super casual, you could say cheers, take care or talk to you soon. Some more professional options are best regards or all the best or simply thank you. For very official documents or formal emails, you can say sincerely. I'm asking Jacob for a favor so I'm going to just say thank you. Keep it simple. Finally, our last step. Step seven is your signature. You write your name and maybe your email address or your company info afterwards.

Definitely. We'll do. Thank you so much Gem and I'll talk to you again soon. That was a great meeting with Gem and now I'm going to record an MP3 file because that was at the top of my to-do list and I want to knock it out right away.
